content:
**Executive summary:** **Light** is a form of energy that enables vision. It travels in **straight lines** at about **3 × 10⁸ m/s** in air/vacuum — the fastest speed in nature — and needs **no medium**, unlike sound.

**Must know**
• Light may be **reflected** (bounced back), **refracted** (bent on entering a new medium), or **absorbed** when it meets a surface.
• **Rectilinear propagation** — light travels in straight lines; this explains shadows and pinhole images.
• Contrast with **sound**: sound is a mechanical wave needing a medium; light crosses the vacuum from the Sun to Earth.

**Chapter roadmap**
• **Refraction** — bending between media and everyday illusions (bent pencil, shallow pool).
• **Spherical mirrors** — concave/convex, image formation, uses.
• **Dispersion** — prism, VIBGYOR spectrum, rainbow.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Light vs sound (bell-jar logic)**

| Property | Light | Sound |
|---|---|---|
| Needs medium? | **No** — travels in vacuum | **Yes** — mechanical wave |
| Speed in air | ~3 × 10⁸ m/s | ~340 m/s |
| Bell-jar test | Hammer still **visible** | Sound **fades** as air removed |

**Cornell cues**
• What is light? → Energy producing sensation of vision
• Fastest speed in nature? → 3 × 10⁸ m/s in vacuum/air
• Three things light can do at a boundary? → Reflect, refract, absorb
• Why astronauts use radio on Moon? → No air for sound; light/radio need no medium

**Definitions / Exam traps**
• **Light** — form of energy that produces the sensation of vision.
• **Exam trap** — "Light is a mechanical wave" is **FALSE**; only sound is mechanical at this level.
• **Exam trap** — Do not confuse **reflection** (bounce back) with **refraction** (bend while passing through).

**🔗 Links:** phy-s5-sec_02, phy-s7-sec_01
teacherTip: Open with the bell-jar contrast: students see the hammer but cannot hear it — that single demo anchors the whole chapter.
examTip: Memorise one-line definitions: reflection = bounces back; refraction = bends while passing through; dispersion = splits white light into colours.

subtopic: 2. Refraction of Light and Its Effects
content:
**Executive summary:** **Refraction** is the **bending of light** when it passes **obliquely** from one transparent medium to another, caused by a **change in the speed of light**.

**Must know**
• **Rarer → denser** (air → water/glass): bends **towards the normal**; angle of refraction **r < i**.
• **Denser → rarer** (water/glass → air): bends **away from the normal**; **r > i**.
• **Normal incidence** (along the normal): **no bending** — ray only slows or speeds up.
• Speed order: fastest in **air/vacuum**, slower in **water**, slowest in **glass** (among common media).

**Everyday effects (one cause — rays bend away from normal on leaving water)**
• Pencil/stick dipped in water appears **bent at the surface**.
• Pool/tank appears **shallower** than it is — **apparent depth < real depth** (dangerous for divers).
• Coin at bottom appears **raised**; invisible coin becomes visible when water is poured in.
• Fish appears **closer to the surface** — spear **below** the image to hit it.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Reflection vs refraction**

| | Reflection | Refraction |
|---|---|---|
| What happens | Light **bounces back** | Light **bends** while crossing boundary |
| Cause | Surface polish | **Change of speed** in new medium |
| Examples | Mirror image | Bent pencil, shallow pool |
| At normal incidence | Ray returns along normal | **No bending** (only speed changes) |

**📊 Chart — Bending rules**

| Crossing | Bending | Angle relation |
|---|---|---|
| Air → glass/water (rarer → denser) | Towards normal | r < i |
| Glass/water → air (denser → rarer) | Away from normal | r > i |

**Cornell cues**
• Definition of refraction? → Oblique passage → bending between transparent media
• Why refraction happens? → Speed of light changes between media
• Pool looks shallow because? → Rays from floor bend away on leaving water; eye traces back higher
• Tank deeper or shallower than real? → **Shallower** (apparent depth < real depth)

**Definitions / Exam traps**
• **Refraction** — bending of light passing obliquely from one transparent medium to another.
• **Exam trap** — "Tank appears **deeper**" is FALSE; refraction makes it look **shallower**.
• **Exam trap** — Assertion–reason favourite: A (light bends) + R (speed changes) → both true, R explains A.

**🔗 Links:** phy-s5-sec_03, phy-s5-sec_07

**From linked questions (15 Qs):**
• Refraction is the change in direction (bending) of light when it passes obliquely from one transparent medium to another, caused by the change in the speed of light between the media.
• Glass is optically denser than air; light slows down on entering it and bends TOWARDS the normal.
• At normal incidence the angle of incidence is 0°.
• Rays from the under-water part of the pencil refract (bend away from the normal) as they pass from water to air.
• Light from the pool floor bends away from the normal as it leaves the water.
• Rays from the coin, on passing from water to air, bend away from the normal.
• Refraction is fundamentally caused by the change in the speed of light between media (light is slower in water than air).
• Rays from the fish refract away from the normal at the surface; traced back, they appear to come from a point above the fish's real position (apparent depth < real depth).

**From linked questions (15 Qs):**
• Refraction is the change in direction (bending) of light when it passes obliquely from one transparent medium to another, caused by the change in the speed of light between the media.
• Glass is optically denser than air; light slows down on entering it and bends TOWARDS the normal.
• At normal incidence the angle of incidence is 0°.
• Rays from the under-water part of the pencil refract (bend away from the normal) as they pass from water to air.
• Light from the pool floor bends away from the normal as it leaves the water.
• Rays from the coin, on passing from water to air, bend away from the normal.
• Refraction is fundamentally caused by the change in the speed of light between media (light is slower in water than air).
• Rays from the fish refract away from the normal at the surface; traced back, they appear to come from a point above the fish's real position (apparent depth < real depth).
teacherTip: Draw one ray diagram for a coin in water: one diagram explains pencil, pool, fish and raised coin.
examTip: Always measure angles from the **normal**, never from the surface — mislabelled angles lose marks even if bending direction is correct.

content:
**Executive summary:** Every refraction diagram needs the **incident ray**, **normal**, **refracted ray**, and the two angles **i** and **r** — all measured from the **normal**.

**Must know**
• **Incident ray** — ray approaching the boundary (e.g. AO).
• **Point of incidence** — O, where ray meets the boundary.
• **Normal** — line perpendicular to the boundary at O (draw as **dotted**).
• **Refracted ray** — bent ray in the second medium (e.g. OB).
• **Angle of incidence (i)** — between incident ray and normal.
• **Angle of refraction (r)** — between refracted ray and normal.
• Incident ray, normal and refracted ray lie in the **same plane**.

**Optically denser / rarer**
• **Optically denser** medium slows light more (glass > water > air).
• Optical density refers to **speed of light**, not mass density — though they usually go together.
• Quick check: air → water gives **r < i**; water → air gives **r > i**.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Refraction vocabulary**

| Term | Symbol / label | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Incident ray | AO | Approaching the boundary |
| Point of incidence | O | Where ray hits boundary |
| Normal | N₁N₂ | Perpendicular at O |
| Refracted ray | OB | Ray in second medium |
| Angle of incidence | i | Between incident ray & normal |
| Angle of refraction | r | Between refracted ray & normal |

**Cornell cues**
• Angle of refraction measured from? → **Normal**, not the surface
• Glass vs air — which is optically denser? → Glass (light slower)
• Same plane rule? → Incident ray, normal, refracted ray coplanar

**Definitions / Exam traps**
• **Optically denser medium** — medium in which light travels **slower**.
• **Exam trap** — "Optically denser = heavier" is misleading; it is about **speed of light**.
• **Exam trap** — Confusing angle with the **surface** instead of the **normal** is the commonest diagram error.

**🔗 Links:** phy-s5-sec_02, phy-s5-sec_09

**From linked questions (1 Qs):**
• Both the angle of incidence and the angle of refraction are always measured from the NORMAL (the perpendicular to the surface at the point of incidence), not from the surface itself.

**From linked questions (1 Qs):**
• Both the angle of incidence and the angle of refraction are always measured from the NORMAL (the perpendicular to the surface at the point of incidence), not from the surface itself.
teacherTip: Insist students label i and r with arcs touching the normal before anything else — examiners check arc position first.
examTip: State 'angles measured from the normal' in every refraction answer; it signals you know the convention.

content:
**Executive summary:** A **spherical mirror** is part of a hollow sphere with one polished reflecting surface. Know **P, C, F, R, f** and the relation **f = R/2**.

**Must know**
• **Concave mirror** — reflecting surface curved **inward** (inside of spoon); **converges** light.
• **Convex mirror** — surface bulges **outward**; **diverges** light.
• **Pole (P)** — midpoint of the reflecting surface.
• **Centre of curvature (C)** — centre of the sphere of which the mirror is a part.
• **Radius of curvature (R)** — distance PC.
• **Principal axis** — straight line through P and C.
• **Principal focus (F)** — parallel rays meet here (concave: **real**, in front) or appear to come from here (convex: **virtual**, behind).
• **Focal length (f)** — distance PF; **f = R/2** (F midway between P and C).
• **Aperture** — width of the reflecting surface.

**Finding f experimentally:** focus sunlight on paper to smallest brightest spot — mirror-to-spot distance = **f** (sun's rays are parallel).
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Concave vs convex mirror (terms)**

| Feature | Concave | Convex |
|---|---|---|
| Shape | Curved **inward** | Bulges **outward** |
| Action on parallel rays | **Converges** to real F in front | **Diverges**; virtual F behind |
| Principal focus | **Real** (in front) | **Virtual** (behind) |
| f and R | f = R/2 | f = R/2 |

**Cornell cues**
• f in terms of R? → **f = R/2**
• R = 30 cm → f? → **15 cm**
• Which mirror converges? → Concave
• Sunny-day focal length test? → Smallest bright spot on paper

**Definitions / Exam traps**
• **Principal focus** — point on principal axis where rays parallel to axis meet (concave) or appear to meet (convex) after reflection.
• **Exam trap** — Forgetting to halve R before ray diagrams (R = 30 cm ⇒ f = 15 cm).
• **Exam trap** — Pole is centre of **reflecting surface**, not centre of curvature.

**🔗 Links:** phy-s5-sec_06, phy-s5-sec_08

**From linked questions (7 Qs):**
• The principal focus lies exactly midway between the pole and the centre of curvature, so focal length = half the radius of curvature (f = R/2).
• A concave mirror is a converging mirror: parallel rays meet at the principal focus in front of the mirror — a REAL focus.
• The point where rays parallel to the principal axis actually meet after reflection from a concave mirror is the PRINCIPAL FOCUS (F).
• T/F: "The focal length of a spherical mirror is half its radius of curvature." → **true** — True.
• T/F: "The centre of the reflecting surface of a spherical mirror is called its pole." → **true** — True.
• Fill: **focus** — Principal focus (F) — real for a concave mirror, virtual for a convex mirror; f = R/2.

**From linked questions (7 Qs):**
• The principal focus lies exactly midway between the pole and the centre of curvature, so focal length = half the radius of curvature (f = R/2).
• A concave mirror is a converging mirror: parallel rays meet at the principal focus in front of the mirror — a REAL focus.
• The point where rays parallel to the principal axis actually meet after reflection from a concave mirror is the PRINCIPAL FOCUS (F).
• T/F: "The focal length of a spherical mirror is half its radius of curvature." → **true** — True.
• T/F: "The centre of the reflecting surface of a spherical mirror is called its pole." → **true** — True.
• Fill: **focus** — Principal focus (F) — real for a concave mirror, virtual for a convex mirror; f = R/2.
teacherTip: Use a spoon: concave = inside, convex = outside. Let students measure f with sunlight — the charred paper spot makes the focus unforgettable.
examTip: On every mirror diagram label **P, C, F** and write **f = R/2** before drawing rays.

subtopic: 5. Images by Concave and Convex Mirrors; Uses
content:
**Executive summary:** A **convex mirror always** gives a **virtual, erect, diminished** image behind the mirror. A **concave mirror** can form **real or virtual** images depending on object position.

**Must know — convex mirror**
• For **any** object position: image is **virtual, erect, diminished**, behind mirror.
• Uses: **rear-view/side mirrors** (wide field of view; objects look **farther** than they are), **security mirrors** at shop corners.

**Must know — concave mirror (by object position)**
• Object far/beyond C → **real, inverted, diminished**.
• Object within focal length (between P and F) → **virtual, erect, magnified** (shaving/dentist mirror).

**Uses of concave mirrors**
• **Shaving/make-up mirror, dentist's mirror** — face/tooth inside F.
• **Torch, car headlight, searchlight** — bulb at **focus** → parallel beam (reverse of image formation).
• **Solar furnace/cooker** — converges sunlight to focus.
• **Doctors' head mirrors (ENT)** — converge light onto throat/ear.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Concave vs convex mirror (images & uses)**

| Mirror | Image (any/normal position) | Key use | Why chosen |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Convex** | Virtual, erect, **diminished** | Rear-view mirror | **Wide field of view** |
| **Concave** (object inside F) | Virtual, erect, **magnified** | Shaving, dentist | Enlarged view |
| **Concave** (bulb at F) | Parallel reflected beam | Headlight, torch | Intense straight beam |
| **Concave** (sun parallel) | Converges to focus | Solar cooker | Heat at focus |

**Cornell cues**
• Convex mirror magnified? → **Never** — always diminished
• Dentist mirror type and position? → Concave; object between P and F
• Car headlight reflector? → Concave; bulb at **focus**
• Why convex for rear-view? → Small erect image = wide angle view

**Definitions / Exam traps**
• **Exam trap** — "Convex mirror forms magnified images" is FALSE (Assertion A true, Reason R false type).
• **Exam trap** — Convex mirror: vehicles appear **farther** than actual — safety caution.
• **Exam trap** — Match-the-column: convex ↔ rear-view; concave ↔ shaving, dentist, headlight, solar cooker.

**🔗 Links:** phy-s5-sec_07, phy-s5-sec_08

**From linked questions (8 Qs):**
• Whatever the object's position, a convex mirror forms a virtual, erect, diminished image behind the mirror.
• When an object lies between the pole and focus of a concave mirror, the image is virtual, erect and MAGNIFIED — ideal for examining teeth.
• A convex mirror diverges light, forming small erect images of everything behind the vehicle, so a large region fits into a small mirror (wide field of view).
• A is true, but R is false — a convex mirror always forms DIMINISHED (smaller) images, never magnified.
• I and II are correct: a concave mirror forms real images for objects beyond F and a virtual magnified image for objects within F; parallel rays really converge at its focus.
• T/F: "A convex mirror can form a real image of a normal object placed in front of it." → **false** — False.
• T/F: "A concave mirror used as a shaving mirror forms a magnified, erect image when the face is held between the pole and the focus." → **true** — True.

**From linked questions (8 Qs):**
• Whatever the object's position, a convex mirror forms a virtual, erect, diminished image behind the mirror.
• When an object lies between the pole and focus of a concave mirror, the image is virtual, erect and MAGNIFIED — ideal for examining teeth.
• A convex mirror diverges light, forming small erect images of everything behind the vehicle, so a large region fits into a small mirror (wide field of view).
• A is true, but R is false — a convex mirror always forms DIMINISHED (smaller) images, never magnified.
• I and II are correct: a concave mirror forms real images for objects beyond F and a virtual magnified image for objects within F; parallel rays really converge at its focus.
• T/F: "A convex mirror can form a real image of a normal object placed in front of it." → **false** — False.
• T/F: "A concave mirror used as a shaving mirror forms a magnified, erect image when the face is held between the pole and the focus." → **true** — True.
teacherTip: One-line logic: convex always diverges → always small virtual erect; concave converges → can do everything depending on object position.
examTip: Match-the-columns on mirror uses is near-certain — drill the five pairs until automatic.

content:
**Executive summary:** A **real image** is formed by the **actual meeting** of rays and can be caught on a screen; a **virtual image** is where rays only **appear** to meet — no screen possible.

**Must know — real image**
• Formed by **actual intersection** of reflected/refracted rays.
• **Can be obtained on a screen**.
• Always **inverted**.
• Examples: distant building in concave mirror; cinema screen.

**Must know — virtual image**
• Formed where rays only **appear to meet** when traced backwards.
• **Cannot** be caught on a screen — no light actually passes through image position.
• Always **erect**.
• Examples: plane mirror face; convex mirror images; magnified face in shaving mirror.

**Three differences to quote**
1. Actual vs apparent meeting of rays.
2. Screen vs no screen.
3. Inverted vs erect.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Real vs virtual image**

| Property | Real image | Virtual image |
|---|---|---|
| Ray meeting | **Actual** intersection | **Apparent** (traced back) |
| Screen | **Can** be obtained | **Cannot** be obtained |
| Orientation | Always **inverted** | Always **erect** |
| Light at image point | Rays **actually pass** | No light **actually arrives** |
| Mirror rule (this level) | Real ⇒ inverted | Virtual ⇒ erect |

**Cornell cues**
• Sure test for real image? → Can be obtained on a **screen**
• Why no screen for virtual? → No light actually reaches that point
• Cinema screen image — real or virtual? → **Real**
• Your face in plane mirror? → **Virtual**, erect

**Definitions / Exam traps**
• **Real image** — formed by actual meeting of light rays after reflection/refraction.
• **Virtual image** — formed where reflected/refracted rays appear to meet when produced backwards.
• **Exam trap** — "Real ⇒ inverted, virtual ⇒ erect" holds for **all mirror images** at this level — use as consistency check.
• **Exam trap** — A–R favourite: virtual image cannot be screened because no light meets there — R explains A.

**🔗 Links:** phy-s5-sec_06, phy-s5-sec_08

**From linked questions (5 Qs):**
• A real image is formed by the ACTUAL meeting of reflected rays, so it can be caught on a screen and is always inverted.
• Since no actual light rays pass through the position of a virtual image, a screen placed there receives no light and shows nothing.
• T/F: "A real image is always inverted with respect to the object." → **true** — True.
• Fill: **real** — Real image — formed by the actual meeting of light rays; it is always inverted.
• 1.

**From linked questions (5 Qs):**
• A real image is formed by the ACTUAL meeting of reflected rays, so it can be caught on a screen and is always inverted.
• Since no actual light rays pass through the position of a virtual image, a screen placed there receives no light and shows nothing.
• T/F: "A real image is always inverted with respect to the object." → **true** — True.
• Fill: **real** — Real image — formed by the actual meeting of light rays; it is always inverted.
• 1.
teacherTip: Place a screen behind a plane mirror — nothing appears. Then use a concave mirror with a distant object — inverted image on screen. The contrast sticks.
examTip: 'Real ⇒ inverted, virtual ⇒ erect' — write this at the top of every image-formation answer as a free check.

content:
**Executive summary:** As an object walks **in** from infinity toward a concave mirror, the **real image walks out** from F past C, growing all the time; once the object crosses **inside F**, the image flips **behind** the mirror — virtual, erect, magnified.

**Must know — object position table**
• Object at **infinity** → image at **F**: real, inverted, highly diminished (point-like).
• Object **beyond C** → image between C and F: real, inverted, **diminished**.
• Object **at C** → image at C: real, inverted, **same size**.
• Object **between C and F** → image beyond C: real, inverted, **magnified**.
• Object **at F** → reflected rays **parallel**; image at **infinity** (torch/headlight principle in reverse).
• Object **between P and F** → image behind mirror: **virtual, erect, magnified**.

**Ray-diagram shortcut:** (1) Ray parallel to axis → reflects through F. (2) Ray through C → returns along itself. Intersection = image.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Concave mirror: object position → image**

| Object position | Image position | Nature | Size |
|---|---|---|---|
| At infinity | At F | Real, inverted | Highly diminished |
| Beyond C | Between C and F | Real, inverted | Diminished |
| At C | At C | Real, inverted | Same size |
| Between C and F | Beyond C | Real, inverted | Magnified |
| At F | At infinity | — | Parallel rays emerge |
| Between P and F | Behind mirror | Virtual, erect | Magnified |

**Cornell cues**
• Same-size real image — where is object? → **At C**
• Object at F — what happens to reflected rays? → Emerge **parallel**
• Headlight bulb position? → At **focus** of concave reflector
• Shaving mirror — object where? → Between **P and F**

**Definitions / Exam traps**
• **Exam trap** — Object at F: image "at infinity" — not a screen image; this is searchlight/torch arrangement.
• **Exam trap** — Headlight: bulb at **F**, not at C (at C rays converge back, not parallel beam).
• **Exam trap** — Learn table as a **story** (object walks in, image walks out) — reconstruct any row in exam.

**🔗 Links:** phy-s5-sec_05, phy-s5-sec_06, phy-s5-sec_07

**From linked questions (5 Qs):**
• With the object at the centre of curvature (C), the concave mirror forms a real, inverted image of equal size, also at C.
• Rays from the focus, after reflection, travel parallel to the principal axis — the image is said to form at infinity.
• With the bulb at the FOCUS of a concave reflector, rays after reflection emerge PARALLEL, throwing an intense straight beam far down the road.
• Hold the concave mirror facing the sun, and move a small sheet of paper in front of it until the sunlight reflected by the mirror converges to the smallest, brightest spot on the paper.

**From linked questions (5 Qs):**
• With the object at the centre of curvature (C), the concave mirror forms a real, inverted image of equal size, also at C.
• Rays from the focus, after reflection, travel parallel to the principal axis — the image is said to form at infinity.
• With the bulb at the FOCUS of a concave reflector, rays after reflection emerge PARALLEL, throwing an intense straight beam far down the road.
• Hold the concave mirror facing the sun, and move a small sheet of paper in front of it until the sunlight reflected by the mirror converges to the smallest, brightest spot on the paper.
teacherTip: Tell the 'walking story': object approaches from infinity — watch the image grow and move out until it jumps behind the mirror at F.
examTip: For sunny-day focal length: parallel sun rays → converge at F; distance to brightest spot = f.

subtopic: 8. The Prism: Dispersion and the Rainbow
content:
**Executive summary:** A **prism** separates white light into **VIBGYOR** (**dispersion**) because different colours travel at **different speeds in glass** — violet deviates **most**, red **least**. The prism **does not create** colours.

**Must know**
• **Dispersion** — splitting of white light into seven constituent colours.
• Coloured band = **spectrum**; order = **VIBGYOR** (Violet, Indigo, Blue, Green, Yellow, Orange, Red).
• Violet slowed most → **deviates most**; red slowed least → **deviates least**.
• Deviation towards the **base** of the prism.
• **Glass slab** — parallel faces → colours recombine → no visible spectrum.
• **Prism** — inclined faces → separation increases.

**Rainbow formation**
• Millions of **raindrops** act as tiny prisms.
• Sunlight: **refracted** on entry → **dispersed** → **internally reflected** at back → **refracted** again on leaving.
• Seen when **Sun is behind** the observer and rain is in front (~40–42°).
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Prism vs glass slab**

| | Triangular prism | Rectangular glass slab |
|---|---|---|
| Refracting surfaces | **Inclined** | **Parallel** |
| Spectrum visible? | **Yes** — VIBGYOR band | **No** — colours recombine |
| Why | Separation increases | First-surface split undone at second surface |

**📊 Chart — Colour deviation in prism**

| Colour | Speed in glass | Deviation |
|---|---|---|
| **Red** | Fastest | **Least** |
| **Violet** | Slowest | **Most** |
| Order (most → least deviation) | Violet … Red | Reverse of VIBGYOR read aloud |

**Cornell cues**
• Dispersion definition? → Splitting white light into seven colours
• Prism creates colours? → **No** — only **separates** existing colours
• Least deviated colour? → **Red**
• Rainbow — where must Sun be? → **Behind** observer

**Definitions / Exam traps**
• **Dispersion** — splitting of white light into its constituent colours by a prism.
• **Spectrum** — band of seven colours (VIBGYOR).
• **Exam trap** — "Prism adds colour to white light" is FALSE.
• **Exam trap** — A–R: violet deviates more because it travels slower in glass — R explains A.

**🔗 Links:** phy-s5-sec_10, phy-s5-sec_02

**From linked questions (11 Qs):**
• Dispersion is the separation of white light into its colours (VIBGYOR).
• Red light travels fastest in glass among the visible colours and deviates the least; violet travels slowest and deviates the most.
• Each raindrop acts like a tiny prism: sunlight refracts on entering the drop, is internally reflected at its back surface, and refracts again on leaving — getting dispersed into VIBGYOR.
• In a prism the refracting surfaces are inclined, so the separation of colours increases at the second surface.
• The slower a colour travels in glass, the more it bends.
• I and III are correct.
• T/F: "In dispersion through a prism, violet light deviates the most." → **true** — True.
• Fill: **dispersion** — Dispersion — seen when white light passes through a prism, or through raindrops forming a rainbow.

**From linked questions (11 Qs):**
• Dispersion is the separation of white light into its colours (VIBGYOR).
• Red light travels fastest in glass among the visible colours and deviates the least; violet travels slowest and deviates the most.
• Each raindrop acts like a tiny prism: sunlight refracts on entering the drop, is internally reflected at its back surface, and refracts again on leaving — getting dispersed into VIBGYOR.
• In a prism the refracting surfaces are inclined, so the separation of colours increases at the second surface.
• The slower a colour travels in glass, the more it bends.
• I and III are correct.
• T/F: "In dispersion through a prism, violet light deviates the most." → **true** — True.
• Fill: **dispersion** — Dispersion — seen when white light passes through a prism, or through raindrops forming a rainbow.
teacherTip: Use a CD in sunlight for dispersion, then prism — students see the prism does not 'paint' new colours, only sorts them.
examTip: Two giveaway facts: red deviates least / violet most; prism does not create colours.

content:
**Executive summary:** Two classic proofs show **white light is a mixture of seven colours**: Newton's **inverted second prism** (recombination in glass) and **Newton's colour disc** (recombination in the eye by **persistence of vision**).

**Must know — inverted second prism**
• First prism disperses white light into VIBGYOR.
• Identical prism placed **upside-down** bends each colour by equal and opposite amount.
• Colours **recombine** → white light emerges.
• Conclusion: prism **separates**; it does not manufacture colours.

**Must know — Newton's colour disc**
• Disc painted with seven rainbow colours in sectors.
• Spun rapidly → appears **nearly white** (whitish grey with impure paints).
• **Persistence of vision** — eye retains each colour impression briefly; rapid succession blends into white sensation.
• Reverse of dispersion, but happening **inside the eye**.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Two recombination experiments**

| Experiment | Where recombination happens | Key idea |
|---|---|---|
| **Inverted second prism** | In **glass** | Equal & opposite bending undoes dispersion |
| **Newton's colour disc** | In the **eye** | Persistence of vision merges colours |

**Cornell cues**
• Second prism orientation? → **Inverted** (upside-down)
• Spinning colour disc looks? → Nearly **white**
• Persistence of vision? → Eye holds image ~1/16 s — colours blend when fast
• Honest observation for disc? → **Whitish grey** (impure pigments) — stating this wins practical marks

**Definitions / Exam traps**
• **Persistence of vision** — eye retains sensation of an image for a short time after it disappears.
• **Exam trap** — Pair experiments in answers: prism-2 = in glass; colour disc = in eye.
• **Exam trap** — Disc never looks pure white with paints — say 'whitish' for full credit.

**🔗 Links:** phy-s5-sec_09, phy-s5-sec_01

**From linked questions (2 Qs):**
• The spinning disc presents each colour to the eye so quickly that the impressions merge (persistence of vision), recombining the colours into the sensation of white.
• The inverted second prism bends the separated colours by equal and opposite amounts, bringing them back together to emerge as white light.

**From linked questions (2 Qs):**
• The spinning disc presents each colour to the eye so quickly that the impressions merge (persistence of vision), recombining the colours into the sensation of white.
• The inverted second prism bends the separated colours by equal and opposite amounts, bringing them back together to emerge as white light.
teacherTip: Pair the two demos in one lesson: disperse with prism 1, recombine with prism 2 — then spin the disc for the 'eye version'.
examTip: Exam answer template: Experiment 1 (inverted prism, glass) + Experiment 2 (colour disc, persistence of vision).

subtopic: 1. Thermal Expansion in Solids and Its Applications
content:
**Executive summary:** Solids **expand on heating** and **contract on cooling** because molecules vibrate more and push slightly farther apart — molecules themselves do not grow.

**Must know — three kinds of expansion**
• **Linear** (length), **superficial** (area), **cubical** (volume).
• If α, β, γ are coefficients: **β = 2α**, **γ = 3α** (α : β : γ = **1 : 2 : 3**).
• Different solids expand **differently** (brass > iron); **INVAR** (Fe–Ni alloy) has **negligible expansion** — precision tapes, clock pendulums.

**Allowing for expansion**
• **Gaps between rails**; expansion joints in bridges/concrete roads; **bridge girders on rollers**.
• Wires strung **sagging** in summer (winter contraction would snap taut wires).
• Gaps at ends of steel girders and around glass window panes.

**Using expansion deliberately**
• **Iron rim** — heated, slipped on wheel, cooled to grip (shrink fitting).
• **Red-hot rivets** — hammered in; contraction clamps plates.
• Hot water on metal lid — metal expands more than glass, lid loosens.
• **Bimetallic strip** — brass + iron; unequal expansion bends strip → thermostats, electric irons, fire alarms.

**Problems caused**
• Thick glass cracks with boiling water (unequal layer expansion).
• Pendulum clock **loses time in summer** (longer rod → slower swing); **gains in winter**. INVAR pendulum fixes this.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Solid vs liquid vs gas expansion (preview)**

| State | Expansion for same ΔT | Substance-specific? |
|---|---|---|
| **Solid** | Least | **Yes** — brass > iron |
| **Liquid** | More than solid | **Yes** — alcohol > water |
| **Gas** | **Greatest** | **No** — all gases ~equal |

**📊 Chart — Applications of solid expansion**

| Strategy | Example | Physics |
|---|---|---|
| Leave room | Rail gaps, bridge rollers | Prevent buckling/snapping |
| Exploit contraction | Iron rim, rivets | Shrink fit grips |
| Unequal expansion | Bimetallic strip, glass cracking | Different expansivities |

**Cornell cues**
• Ball-and-ring experiment shows? → Solid **cubical expansion** on heating
• INVAR used because? → **Negligible** thermal expansion
• Pendulum slow in summer why? → Rod **lengthens** → longer period
• β and γ in terms of α? → β = 2α, γ = 3α

**Definitions / Exam traps**
• **Linear expansivity (α)** — fractional increase in length per °C rise.
• **Exam trap** — Give **season + direction** in 'give reason' answers (expands in summer / contracts in winter).
• **Exam trap** — Thick glass cracks: inner layer expands first, outer stays cool — unequal expansion.

**🔗 Links:** phy-s6-sec_05, phy-s6-sec_07

**From linked questions (28 Qs):**
• Solids expand on heating.
• Wires contract on cooling.
• Heating expands the rim enough to slip over the wheel.
• Heating increases the ball's volume (cubical expansion), so it can no longer pass through the ring.
• Metals expand more than glass for the same temperature rise.
• Glass is a poor conductor of heat.
• Different metals have different expansivities.
• The period of a pendulum increases with its length.

**From linked questions (28 Qs):**
• Solids expand on heating.
• Wires contract on cooling.
• Heating expands the rim enough to slip over the wheel.
• Heating increases the ball's volume (cubical expansion), so it can no longer pass through the ring.
• Metals expand more than glass for the same temperature rise.
• Glass is a poor conductor of heat.
• Different metals have different expansivities.
• The period of a pendulum increases with its length.
teacherTip: Three moves cover almost every application: leave room, exploit contraction, exploit unequal expansion — teach the taxonomy, not isolated examples.
examTip: Ball-and-ring demo is the visual anchor; follow with bimetallic strip bending in a candle flame.

subtopic: 2. Expansion of Liquids; the Water Anomaly
content:
**Executive summary:** Liquids have no definite shape — only **cubical (volume) expansion** matters. Water shows **anomalous expansion** between 0 °C and 4 °C, with **maximum density at 4 °C**.

**Must know — liquids generally**
• Liquids expand **more than solids** for the same temperature rise.
• **Different liquids expand differently** (alcohol >> water).
• **Thermometer** — mercury expands regularly up fine capillary.
• Flask-and-tube: level first **dips** (glass expands first, enlarging capacity), then **rises** as liquid expansion dominates.

**Anomalous expansion of water**
• 0 °C → 4 °C: water **contracts** on heating.
• Above 4 °C: expands normally.
• **Maximum density at 4 °C** (1 g/cm³); ice less dense (R.D. ≈ 0.9) — water **expands on freezing** (~1/11 volume).

**Consequences**
• **Ice floats** on water.
• **Ponds freeze at top only** — 4 °C water sinks to bottom; colder lighter water rises and freezes at surface; ice insulates below — **fish survive**.
• **Water pipes burst** in severe winters; sealed bottle cracks in freezer.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Normal liquid vs water (anomaly)**

| Temperature change | Normal liquid | Water |
|---|---|---|
| Heating from cold | Volume **increases** | 0→4 °C: volume **decreases** |
| Density on heating | Decreases steadily | **Maximum at 4 °C** |
| On freezing | Usually contracts | **Expands** (~1/11) — ice floats |

**📊 Chart — Flask-and-tube experiment**

| Stage | What expands first | Liquid level |
|---|---|---|
| Just placed in hot water | **Glass** flask | **Dips** (capacity grows) |
| Shortly after | **Water** (more than glass) | **Rises** well above start |

**Cornell cues**
• Only meaningful liquid expansion? → **Cubical / volume**
• Water max density at? → **4 °C**
• Why fish survive frozen pond? → 4 °C water at bottom; ice on top insulates
• Alcohol vs water expansion? → Alcohol expands **more**

**Definitions / Exam traps**
• **Anomalous expansion** — water contracts when heated from 0 °C to 4 °C (opposite to normal).
• **Exam trap** — Keep ranges separate: 0→4 °C contracts; above 4 °C expands normally.
• **Exam trap** — Draw pond diagram: ice top, 4 °C bottom, fish swimming — often worth marks even when not asked.

**🔗 Links:** phy-s6-sec_01, phy-s6-sec_07

**From linked questions (16 Qs):**
• Linear and superficial expansion require a definite shape (length, surface).
• The mercury in the bulb expands up the fine capillary as temperature rises (and contracts on cooling).
• Heat reaches the glass first: the flask expands, increasing its capacity, so the liquid level drops momentarily.
• Different liquids expand differently: e.
• Unlike normal liquids, water CONTRACTS as it warms from 0 °C to 4 °C, reaching maximum density (minimum volume) at 4 °C; beyond 4 °C it expands normally.
• At 4 °C water occupies its minimum volume and hence has maximum density (1 g/cm³).
• As a pond cools, water at 4 °C (maximum density) sinks to the bottom.
• Water is anomalous: on freezing it EXPANDS by about one-eleventh of its volume.

**From linked questions (16 Qs):**
• Linear and superficial expansion require a definite shape (length, surface).
• The mercury in the bulb expands up the fine capillary as temperature rises (and contracts on cooling).
• Heat reaches the glass first: the flask expands, increasing its capacity, so the liquid level drops momentarily.
• Different liquids expand differently: e.
• Unlike normal liquids, water CONTRACTS as it warms from 0 °C to 4 °C, reaching maximum density (minimum volume) at 4 °C; beyond 4 °C it expands normally.
• At 4 °C water occupies its minimum volume and hence has maximum density (1 g/cm³).
• As a pond cools, water at 4 °C (maximum density) sinks to the bottom.
• Water is anomalous: on freezing it EXPANDS by about one-eleventh of its volume.
teacherTip: Flask-and-tube demo is subtle but powerful — predict the dip before showing it; students remember the 'glass wins first' surprise.
examTip: Almost every anomaly question answers with **4 °C** — maximum density, minimum volume.

content:
**Executive summary:** Gases expand the **most** of the three states. For the same temperature rise, **all gases expand nearly equally** — unlike solids and liquids.

**Must know**
• Molecules nearly free → heating speeds them up → strong outward push.
• Expansion order: **solid < liquid << gas**.
• All gases expand **nearly equally** — intermolecular forces negligible in every gas.
• Unlike solids/liquids, expansion is **not substance-specific** for gases.

**Demonstration**
• Balloon on 'empty' (air-filled) flask in hot water → **inflates**; in cold water → **collapses**.

**Everyday examples**
• **Hot-air balloon** — heated air expands, less dense than cold air → rises.
• **Tyres burst** on hot days/long drives — enclosed air cannot expand freely → **pressure** climbs.
• Never heat sealed aerosol cans — trapped gas expands dangerously.

**Key distinction**
• Gas **free** to expand → volume grows (balloon).
• Gas **confined** → pressure grows instead (tyre).
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Expansion: solid vs liquid vs gas**

| State | Relative expansion (same ΔT) | Equal for all substances? |
|---|---|---|
| Solid | **Least** | No — brass ≠ iron |
| Liquid | Intermediate | No — alcohol ≠ water |
| Gas | **Greatest** | **Yes** — all gases ~equal |

**📊 Chart — Free vs confined gas**

| Condition | What increases | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Free** to expand | **Volume** | Balloon on heated flask |
| **Confined** | **Pressure** | Tyre on hot day |

**Cornell cues**
• Least to greatest expansion? → Solid < liquid < gas
• Why all gases expand equally? → Negligible intermolecular forces
• Hot-air balloon rises because? → Heated air less dense than surrounding cold air
• Balloon on flask proves? → Air expands greatly on heating

**Definitions / Exam traps**
• **Exam trap** — True/false favourite: "All gases expand equally, but liquids/solids each in their own way" → **TRUE**.
• **Exam trap** — Spot free vs confined in word problems — that tells you volume vs pressure answer.
• **Exam trap** — 'Empty' bottle is full of **air** — balloon demo proves gas expansion.

**🔗 Links:** phy-s6-sec_01, phy-s6-sec_05

**From linked questions (10 Qs):**
• Solids expand least (molecules tightly bound), liquids expand more, and gases expand the most (negligible intermolecular forces).
• The 'empty' bottle is full of air.
• In solids and liquids, expansion depends on intermolecular forces specific to the substance.
• I and II are correct.
• All three are correctly paired.
• Long, fast driving heats the tyres.
• Heat from the water warms the trapped air, whose molecules move faster and push outward — the air expands greatly (gases expand the most of the three states) and inflates the balloon.
• T/F: "Gases expand much more than solids for the same rise in temperature." → **true** — True.

**From linked questions (10 Qs):**
• Solids expand least (molecules tightly bound), liquids expand more, and gases expand the most (negligible intermolecular forces).
• The 'empty' bottle is full of air.
• In solids and liquids, expansion depends on intermolecular forces specific to the substance.
• I and II are correct.
• All three are correctly paired.
• Long, fast driving heats the tyres.
• Heat from the water warms the trapped air, whose molecules move faster and push outward — the air expands greatly (gases expand the most of the three states) and inflates the balloon.
• T/F: "Gases expand much more than solids for the same rise in temperature." → **true** — True.
teacherTip: Hold a balloon on a flask in hot water — instant gas expansion demo. Contrast with a sealed tyre on a sunny day for confined gas.
examTip: Order chain: solid < liquid << gas — write it at the top of every expansion comparison answer.

subtopic: 1. How Sound Is Produced and How It Travels
content:
**Executive summary:** **Sound is produced by vibrating bodies** and needs a **material medium** (solid, liquid or gas) — it **cannot travel through vacuum**.

**Must know — production**
• Drum skin, plucked string, tuning fork, vocal cords — all **vibrate**.
• Stop vibration (touch ringing bell) → sound stops.

**Must know — propagation**
• **Bell-jar experiment**: air pumped out → sound fades though hammer still **seen** striking; readmit air → sound returns.
• Astronauts on Moon use **radio** — no atmosphere for voice.
• Sound travels as **compressions and rarefactions**; particles vibrate in place and hand disturbance on — they do not travel with the wave.

**Speed of sound**
• Fastest in **solids**, slower in **liquids**, slowest in **gases**.
• Steel ≈ **5000 m/s**, water ≈ **1500 m/s**, air ≈ **340 m/s**.
• Ear on rail hears distant train **earlier and louder** — sound faster and less weakened in solids.

**Lightning before thunder**
• Light ≈ 3 × 10⁸ m/s vs sound ≈ 340 m/s.
• Every **3 s delay ≈ 1 km** distance; 6 s → ~2040 m ≈ 2 km.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Light vs sound propagation**

| | Light | Sound |
|---|---|---|
| Medium needed? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Speed in air | ~3 × 10⁸ m/s | ~340 m/s |
| Bell jar evacuated | Still see hammer | Sound **inaudible** |
| Moon communication | Radio (EM waves) | No direct voice |

**📊 Chart — Speed of sound in media**

| Medium | Approx. speed | Order |
|---|---|---|
| Steel (solid) | ~5000 m/s | **Fastest** |
| Water (liquid) | ~1500 m/s | Intermediate |
| Air (gas) | ~340 m/s | **Slowest** |
| Vacuum | **No propagation** | — |

**Cornell cues**
• Bell jar proves? → Sound needs material medium
• Why hammer still visible? → Light needs no medium
• Ear on railway track? → Sound faster/better in solids
• 3 s flash-to-thunder gap means? → Lightning ~**1 km** away

**Definitions / Exam traps**
• **Sound** — longitudinal mechanical wave produced by vibrating bodies, propagating through a material medium.
• **Exam trap** — Bell-jar = **two marks**: sound fades + hammer visible ⇒ light needs no medium.
• **Exam trap** — "Sound faster in air than steel" is FALSE.

**🔗 Links:** phy-s7-sec_02, phy-s7-sec_03, phy-s5-sec_01

**From linked questions (10 Qs):**
• Every sound source — a drum skin, a guitar string, our vocal cords, a tuning fork — is in vibration.
• The hammer visibly keeps vibrating, so sound is still being produced — but with the air removed there is no medium to carry the vibrations to our ears.
• Sound travels fastest in solids (steel ≈ 5000 m/s), slower in liquids (water ≈ 1500 m/s), and slowest in gases (air ≈ 340 m/s) — because molecules are most closely coupled in solids.
• Light (3 × 10⁸ m/s) reaches us practically instantly; sound crawls at ~340 m/s.
• In solids the closely packed molecules transmit vibrations quickly and efficiently, so sound travels faster (and farther with less loss) through ground or steel rails than through air.
• Sound needs a material medium.
• Sound is a mechanical wave: it travels as successive compressions and rarefactions of the particles of a medium.
• T/F: "Sound can travel through solids, liquids and gases, but not through vacuum." → **true** — True.

**From linked questions (10 Qs):**
• Every sound source — a drum skin, a guitar string, our vocal cords, a tuning fork — is in vibration.
• The hammer visibly keeps vibrating, so sound is still being produced — but with the air removed there is no medium to carry the vibrations to our ears.
• Sound travels fastest in solids (steel ≈ 5000 m/s), slower in liquids (water ≈ 1500 m/s), and slowest in gases (air ≈ 340 m/s) — because molecules are most closely coupled in solids.
• Light (3 × 10⁸ m/s) reaches us practically instantly; sound crawls at ~340 m/s.

teacherTip: Bell-jar demo is the chapter opener — let students predict what happens to sound vs sight as air is removed.
examTip: Lightning–thunder delay: multiply seconds by 340 m (or ÷3 for km) — quick mental math for exams.

subtopic: 2. Frequency, Pitch and the Audible Range
content:
**Executive summary:** **Pitch** (shrillness) depends on **frequency**; **loudness** depends on **amplitude** — these are **independent**.

**Must know — describing vibration**
• **Amplitude** — maximum displacement from mean position.
• **Frequency (f)** — vibrations per second; unit **hertz (Hz)**.
• **Time period (T)** — time for one vibration; **T = 1/f** (reciprocals).

**Pitch and frequency**
• Higher f → shriller; lower f → deeper.
• Mosquito = high f (shrill); lion's roar = low f but large amplitude (loud).
• **Strings**: shorter, tighter, thinner → higher f (guitar frets, tuning pegs).
• **Air columns**: shorter column → higher f (flute holes — open more holes = shorter column = higher note).
• Women/children — shorter, thinner vocal cords → higher pitch.

**Audible range: 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz (20 kHz)**
• Below 20 Hz: **infrasonic** (elephants, earthquakes).
• Above 20 kHz: **ultrasonic** (bats, dogs ~40–50 kHz; dolphins; sonar, medical ultrasound).
• Bats use **echolocation** with ultrasonic squeaks.
• Single fixed frequency = **monotone** (tuning fork).
• **Noise** — unpleasant irregular mixture of frequencies.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Pitch vs loudness**

| Property | Depends on | Changed by | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pitch** (shrillness) | **Frequency** | Shorten string, tighten string, shorten air column | Hz (indirect) |
| **Loudness** | **Amplitude** | Strike harder, larger vibrating area | **decibel (dB)** |
| Independence | Same pitch can be loud or soft | Pluck gently vs strongly | — |

**📊 Chart — Frequency bands**

| Range | Name | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| Below 20 Hz | **Infrasonic** | Elephants, earthquake waves |
| 20 Hz – 20 kHz | **Audible** (human) | Speech, music |
| Above 20 kHz | **Ultrasonic** | Bats, dog whistles, sonar |
| Single fixed f | **Monotone** | Tuning fork |
| Irregular mix | **Noise** | Traffic, unpleasant sound |

**Cornell cues**
• T = 1/f or f = 1/T? → **Reciprocals**
• 20 oscillations in 4 s → f? → **5 Hz**, T = **0.2 s**
• Tighten guitar string → pitch? → **Rises** (higher f)
• Dog hears 'silent' whistle? → **Ultrasonic** — above 20 kHz

**Definitions / Exam traps**
• **Pitch** — shrillness or graveness of sound; determined by frequency.
• **Exam trap** — "Pitch increases with amplitude" is FALSE — that changes loudness only.
• **Exam trap** — 'Supersonic' = faster than sound speed, **not** ultrasonic frequency.

**🔗 Links:** phy-s7-sec_03, phy-s7-sec_05, phy-s7-sec_01

**From linked questions (25 Qs):**
• Frequency is the number of complete vibrations (oscillations) per second; its SI unit is the hertz (Hz).
• Higher frequency → higher pitch (shriller sound); lower frequency → deeper/graver sound.
• For a stretched string, frequency increases as the vibrating length decreases (also as tension increases or thickness decreases).
• Humans hear roughly 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz (20 kHz).
• Ultrasonic sounds lie above 20 kHz — inaudible to humans but heard by bats, dogs and dolphins, and used in medical (ultrasound) scans.
• A monotone is a sound of one fixed frequency, e.
• Shorter, thinner vocal cords vibrate at higher frequencies, giving a higher-pitched (shriller) voice.
• The frequency of a vibrating air column decreases as its length increases.

**From linked questions (25 Qs):**
• Frequency is the number of complete vibrations (oscillations) per second; its SI unit is the hertz (Hz).
• Higher frequency → higher pitch (shriller sound); lower frequency → deeper/graver sound.
• For a stretched string, frequency increases as the vibrating length decreases (also as tension increases or thickness decreases).
• Humans hear roughly 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz (20 kHz).
• Ultrasonic sounds lie above 20 kHz — inaudible to humans but heard by bats, dogs and dolphins, and used in medical (ultrasound) scans.
