teacherTip: Pluck a rubber band gently then hard — same pitch, different loudness. Then tighten it — pitch rises. Two demos, one lesson.
examTip: Memorise trio: pitch↔frequency, loudness↔amplitude, T = 1/f — decides most chapter MCQs.

content:
**Executive summary:** The wave equation **v = f × λ** links **speed**, **frequency** and **wavelength**. Frequency is set by the **source** and does not change with distance.

**Must know**
• **Wavelength (λ)** — distance between two consecutive compressions (or rarefactions); length of one complete wave.
• **v = f λ** — speed = frequency × wavelength.
• Units: f in **Hz**, λ in **m**, v in **m/s**.

**Worked patterns**
• f = 500 Hz, λ = 0.68 m → v = 500 × 0.68 = **340 m/s** (speed of sound in air).
• 20 oscillations in 4 s → f = **5 Hz**, T = **0.2 s**.
• Thunder 6 s after flash → distance = 340 × 6 = **2040 m ≈ 2 km**.
• Rule of thumb: delay (s) × 340 m; every **3 s ≈ 1 km**.

**Checklist**
• Frequency fixed by **source** — unchanged by distance or medium (at source).
• In new medium: **speed and wavelength change**, **frequency stays same**.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Wave quantities**

| Quantity | Symbol | Meaning | SI unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency | f | Vibrations per second | **Hz** |
| Time period | T | Time for one vibration | **s** |
| Wavelength | λ | Crest to crest (compression to compression) | **m** |
| Wave speed | v | f × λ | **m/s** |

**Cornell cues**
• v = fλ rearranged? → f = v/λ, λ = v/f
• f changes with distance? → **No**
• Medium changes — what stays same? → **Frequency** (from source)
• 3 s thunder delay → distance? → ~**1 km**

**Definitions / Exam traps**
• **Wavelength** — distance between two consecutive corresponding points on a wave (e.g. compression to compression).
• **Exam trap** — Rearrange v = fλ **before** substituting numbers — slips happen mid-calculation.
• **Exam trap** — T = 1/f, **not** T = 2f or T = f × 2.

**🔗 Links:** phy-s7-sec_02, phy-s7-sec_01, phy-s7-sec_05

**From linked questions (5 Qs):**
• Wavelength (λ) is the length of one complete wave — e.
• v = f × λ = 500 Hz × 0.
• Distance = speed × time = 340 m/s × 6 s = 2040 m ≈ 2 km.
• Each second of delay means the sound has travelled ~340 m.
• Fill: **wavelength** — v = f × λ — the fundamental wave equation connecting speed, frequency and wavelength.

**From linked questions (5 Qs):**
• Wavelength (λ) is the length of one complete wave — e.
• v = f × λ = 500 Hz × 0.
• Distance = speed × time = 340 m/s × 6 s = 2040 m ≈ 2 km.
• Each second of delay means the sound has travelled ~340 m.
• Fill: **wavelength** — v = f × λ — the fundamental wave equation connecting speed, frequency and wavelength.
teacherTip: Use slinky for λ; count oscillations with stopwatch for f; then calculate v — one hands-on block covers the whole equation.
examTip: Write formula → rearrange → substitute → unit — four lines, four method marks in ICSE numericals.

content:
**Executive summary:** **Loudness depends mainly on amplitude** — in fact loudness ∝ **(amplitude)²**. Pitch and loudness are **independent**.

**Must know — amplitude**
• Strike drum harder → larger amplitude → **louder**, **same pitch**.
• Doubling amplitude → sound **four times** as intense (square law).

**Other factors affecting loudness heard**
• **Distance from source** — energy spreads over growing area; loudness falls (frequency unchanged).
• **Surface area of vibrating body** — tuning fork faint alone; on table much louder (soundboards of guitar, violin, piano).
• **Density of medium** — denser air or wind toward listener carries sound better.
• **Sensitivity of listener's ear**.

**Measuring loudness — decibel (dB)**
• Whisper ≈ 30 dB; conversation ≈ 60 dB; heavy traffic ≈ 80–90 dB.
• Prolonged exposure above ~**80 dB** = **noise pollution**, can damage hearing.
• **Noise** — unpleasant irregular mixture; musical note is regular and pleasant.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Pitch vs loudness (exam favourite)**

| | Pitch | Loudness |
|---|---|---|
| Depends on | **Frequency** | **Amplitude** (mainly) |
| Strike drum harder | Unchanged | **Increases** |
| Move away from bell | Unchanged | **Decreases** |
| Unit (measurement) | Hz (source property) | **decibel (dB)** |
| Relation | — | Loudness ∝ amplitude² |

**📊 Chart — Factors affecting loudness heard**

| Factor | Effect |
|---|---|
| **Amplitude** | Larger → louder (∝ A²) |
| **Distance** | Farther → fainter (energy spread) |
| **Vibrating area** | Larger area → louder (fork on table) |
| **Medium density** | Denser → carries better |
| **Ear sensitivity** | Varies per person |

**Cornell cues**
• Loudness unit? → **Decibel (dB)**
• Frequency changes with distance? → **No** — common A-R trap
• Tuning fork on table? → Larger vibrating area → louder
• 80 dB significance? → **Noise pollution** threshold

**Definitions / Exam traps**
• **Loudness** — sensation of sound intensity; mainly depends on amplitude.
• **Exam trap** — List question wants **four** factors: amplitude, distance, vibrating area, medium density — amplitude alone = partial credit.
• **Exam trap** — A–R: loudness decreases with distance BUT frequency does not — R is false.

**🔗 Links:** phy-s7-sec_02, phy-s7-sec_03, phy-s7-sec_01

**From linked questions (14 Qs):**
• Larger amplitude of vibration → louder sound (loudness is proportional to the SQUARE of the amplitude).
• Loudness decreases with distance from the source: the same sound energy spreads out over an ever-larger area as the wave travels outward, so the energy reaching the ear per second falls.
• Loudness increases with the area of the vibrating surface.
• Loudness is governed by amplitude; pitch by frequency.
• A is true, but R is false: frequency does NOT change with distance — the pitch you hear far away is the same.
• All three are correct.
• Amplitude determines LOUDNESS — a taller wave means a louder sound.
• The only difference between the two graphs is the amplitude (height) of the wave.

**From linked questions (14 Qs):**
• Larger amplitude of vibration → louder sound (loudness is proportional to the SQUARE of the amplitude).
• Loudness decreases with distance from the source: the same sound energy spreads out over an ever-larger area as the wave travels outward, so the energy reaching the ear per second falls.
• Loudness increases with the area of the vibrating surface.
• Loudness is governed by amplitude; pitch by frequency.
• A is true, but R is false: frequency does NOT change with distance — the pitch you hear far away is the same.
• All three are correct.
• Amplitude determines LOUDNESS — a taller wave means a louder sound.
• The only difference between the two graphs is the amplitude (height) of the wave.
teacherTip: Tuning fork on table vs in hand — the single best loudness demo. Pair with wave diagrams showing small vs large amplitude.
examTip: Drum harder = louder not shriller — classic assertion–reason; amplitude up, frequency unchanged.

content:
**Executive summary:** Know the **quantity–unit–instrument** triplets for current, potential difference and resistance, plus **conductors vs insulators**.

**Must know**
• **Electric current** — flow of electric charge; unit **ampere (A)**; measured with **ammeter**.
• **Potential difference (voltage)** — 'electrical pressure' driving current; unit **volt (V)**; measured with **voltmeter**; Indian mains ≈ **220 V**.
• **Resistance** — opposition to current flow; unit **ohm (Ω)**; longer/thinner wires → more resistance; nichrome in heating elements.
• **Conductors** — metals (Cu, Al), graphite, impure water, human body.
• **Insulators** — rubber, glass, dry wood, plastics, porcelain, dry air — wire coverings, plug tops.

**Circuit basics**
• **Closed circuit** needed for current; switch opens/closes path.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Electrical quantities**

| Quantity | Unit | Instrument | Symbol |
|---|---|---|---|
| Electric current | **ampere (A)** | Ammeter | I |
| Potential difference | **volt (V)** | Voltmeter | V |
| Resistance | **ohm (Ω)** | — (this level) | R |
| Electrical power | **watt (W)** | — | P |
| Electrical energy (bill) | **kilowatt-hour (kWh)** | Electricity meter | — |

**Cornell cues**
• Current unit? → **Ampere** (not volt)
• Mains voltage in India? → ~**220 V**
• Thin long wire resistance? → **Higher**
• Human body — conductor or insulator? → **Conductor** (danger with wet skin)

**Definitions / Exam traps**
• **Electric current** — rate of flow of electric charge through a conductor.
• **Potential difference** — work done per unit charge between two points.
• **Exam trap** — Match quantity↔unit is almost guaranteed — learn five pairs.
• **Exam trap** — Ammeter in **series**, voltmeter in **parallel** (if circuit questions appear).

**🔗 Links:** phy-s8-sec_03, phy-s8-sec_05, phy-s8-sec_06

**From linked questions (4 Qs):**
• Current is measured in amperes (A) with an ammeter.
• Fill: **volt** — Volt (V) — measured with a voltmeter; household mains supply in India is about 220 V.
• Fill: **resistance** — Resistance — measured in ohms (Ω); thin and long wires have more resistance.

**From linked questions (4 Qs):**
• Current is measured in amperes (A) with an ammeter.
• Fill: **volt** — Volt (V) — measured with a voltmeter; household mains supply in India is about 220 V.
• Fill: **resistance** — Resistance — measured in ohms (Ω); thin and long wires have more resistance.
teacherTip: Quantity–unit–instrument chant: current–ampere–ammeter; PD–volt–voltmeter; resistance–ohm.
examTip: Match-the-columns on quantity↔unit — write all five pairs once before every test.

subtopic: 2. Power Rating and the Electricity Bill
content:
**Executive summary:** Appliance rating **'220 V, 100 W'** means it consumes **100 J/s** at 220 V. Bills charge for **energy (kWh)**, not power.

**Must know — power ratings**
• **Power (P)** = energy per second; unit **watt (W)**.
• LED 5–10 W, CFL 15–20 W, filament 60–100 W, fan ≈ 75 W, TV ≈ 100 W, iron ≈ 750 W, geyser 1000–2000 W.
• Heating appliances (iron, geyser, heater) are most power-hungry.

**Energy and the 'unit'**
• **Energy = power × time**.
• Commercial unit: **kilowatt-hour (kWh)** — 1 kW for 1 h.
• **1 kWh = 1 unit** on meter = **3.6 × 10⁶ J**.
• **Cost = units × rate per unit**.

**Saving energy**
• **LEDs** vs filament — same light for ~1/10 power (filament wastes most as heat).
• Switch off unused appliances; ISI-marked equipment.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Power vs energy**

| | Power (kW / W) | Energy (kWh) |
|---|---|---|
| Answers | How **fast** energy is used | How **much** was used |
| Unit | **watt** / kilowatt | **kilowatt-hour** |
| On bill? | **No** | **Yes** — 'units' |
| Formula | P = E/t | E = P × t |

**Cornell cues**
• 1 kWh equals? → **1 unit** = 3.6 × 10⁶ J
• 100 W for 10 h? → 0.1 kW × 10 h = **1 unit**
• kW vs kWh? → kW is power; kWh is **energy**
• LED preferred why? → Far less power for same brightness

**Definitions / Exam traps**
• **Power rating** — voltage and power at which appliance is designed to work normally.
• **Exam trap** — '220 V, 100 W' means 100 J/s **at 220 V**, not always 220 J/s.
• **Exam trap** — Cost numerical: W→kW (÷1000), min→h (÷60), then × rate.

**🔗 Links:** phy-s8-sec_23, phy-s8-sec_02, phy-s8-sec_05

**From linked questions (5 Qs):**
• The rating gives the working voltage (220 V) and the power (100 W = 100 joules per second) consumed AT that voltage.
• 1 kilowatt-hour (1 'unit') is the energy consumed by a 1 kW appliance running for 1 hour.
• A filament bulb wastes most of its energy as heat; an LED converts a much larger fraction of electrical energy into light.
• T/F: "1 kWh is the energy consumed by a 1000 W appliance working for one hour." → **true** — True.
• Fill: **unit** — Unit — what the electricity meter records and the bill charges for; 1 unit = 1 kWh.

**From linked questions (5 Qs):**
• The rating gives the working voltage (220 V) and the power (100 W = 100 joules per second) consumed AT that voltage.
• 1 kilowatt-hour (1 'unit') is the energy consumed by a 1 kW appliance running for 1 hour.
• A filament bulb wastes most of its energy as heat; an LED converts a much larger fraction of electrical energy into light.
• T/F: "1 kWh is the energy consumed by a 1000 W appliance working for one hour." → **true** — True.
• Fill: **unit** — Unit — what the electricity meter records and the bill charges for; 1 unit = 1 kWh.
teacherTip: kW vs kWh confusion is universal — use the analogy: kW = speed of spending; kWh = total spent.
examTip: Three-step cost: convert to kW → multiply hours → multiply rate. One step per mark.

content:
**Executive summary:** Household supply has **three wires** — **live**, **neutral** and **earth** — each with a colour code and safety role.

**Must know — three wires**
• **Live wire** — high potential (**≈220 V**); colour **red** (old) / **brown** (IEC); switches and fuses go here.
• **Neutral wire** — completes circuit; nearly **zero potential**; **black** / **blue**.
• **Earth wire** — to metal body of appliances + **plate buried in moist soil**; **green** / **green-yellow**; normally **no current** — only on fault leakage.

**Meter board path**
• Company fuse → **electricity meter** (kWh/units) → main switch → room circuits.

**Three-pin plug**
• Thick top pin = **earth**; **longer** so earth connects **first** before live/neutral.
• Longer and thicker = safety (lowest resistance path for fault current).
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Three household wires**

| Wire | Potential | Colour (old / new) | Normal current? | Function |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Live** | ~220 V | Red / **Brown** | Yes (working) | Brings current in; fuse/switch here |
| **Neutral** | ~0 V | Black / **Blue** | Yes (return) | Completes circuit |
| **Earth** | 0 V (ground) | Green / **Green-yellow** | **No** (only fault) | Safety path for leakage |

**Cornell cues**
• Earth wire carries full working current? → **No** — only on fault
• Why earth pin longest? → Connects **first**; safest path for leakage
• Meter records? → **kWh** (units)
• Both colour conventions? → Quote **old and new** — books differ

**Definitions / Exam traps**
• **Earthing** — connecting metal appliance body to ground via earth wire.
• **Exam trap** — Statement III wrong type: earth wire does NOT carry normal working current.
• **Exam trap** — Switches and fuses must be in **live** wire only.

**🔗 Links:** phy-s8-sec_06, phy-s8-sec_07, phy-s8-sec_02

**From linked questions (4 Qs):**
• I and II are correct.
• T/F: "The earth wire normally carries the working current of an appliance." → **false** — False.
• 1.

**From linked questions (4 Qs):**
• I and II are correct.
• T/F: "The earth wire normally carries the working current of an appliance." → **false** — False.
• 1.
teacherTip: Show a real three-pin plug — students remember earth pin is thick, long, on top when they have held one.
examTip: Quote BOTH colour conventions in every three-wire answer — always safe in ICSE.

subtopic: 4. The Household Circuit: Fuse, MCB and Short Circuit
content:
**Executive summary:** **Fuse** and **MCB** protect the circuit from **overloading** and **short circuit** by breaking the circuit when current exceeds safe limit. Fuse must be in the **live wire**.

**Must know — electric fuse**
• Thin wire of **low-melting-point alloy** (tin–lead) in porcelain holder.
• Excess current heats wire → **melts** → breaks circuit before wiring overheats.
• Always in **LIVE wire** — blown fuse disconnects appliance from high potential.
• **Never** replace with thick copper wire — protection lost.

**MCB (Miniature Circuit Breaker)**
• Modern fuse — automatic switch **trips** on excess current; **reset** without replacing wire.

**Faults protected against**
• **Short circuit** — live touches neutral directly (damaged insulation); resistance ≈ zero → enormous current → fire risk within seconds.
• **Overloading** — too many high-power appliances on one circuit exceed safe current rating.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Fuse vs MCB**

| | **Fuse** | **MCB** |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Thin wire **melts** | Automatic switch **trips** |
| After operation | **Replace** wire | **Reset** switch |
| Wire material | Low melting point alloy | — |
| Placement | **Live wire** | **Live wire** |
| Protects against | Overload + short circuit | Overload + short circuit |

**Cornell cues**
• Fuse wire property? → **Low** melting point
• Fuse in which wire? → **Live**
• Short circuit cause? → Live + neutral touch directly
• Thick copper as fuse? → **Dangerous** — never melts

**Definitions / Exam traps**
• **Short circuit** — live and neutral connected directly with almost zero resistance.
• **Overloading** — total current exceeds circuit's safe rating.
• **Exam trap** — Three fuse facts for full marks: thin wire, **low** melting point, **live** wire.
• **Exam trap** — Fuse in neutral still leaves appliance at 220 V — lethal.

**🔗 Links:** phy-s8-sec_07, phy-s8-sec_05, phy-s8-sec_02

**From linked questions (6 Qs):**
• The live wire carries the high potential (220 V).
• A fuse is a thin wire of low-melting-point alloy (tin–lead).
• A blown fuse in the live wire isolates the appliance from the 220 V supply.
• T/F: "A fuse wire has a low melting point and melts when excessive current flows through it." → **true** — True.
• Fill: **fuse** — Fuse — melts and breaks the circuit when the current exceeds the safe limit (modern homes use MCBs for the same job).
• A short circuit occurs when the live wire touches the neutral wire directly (e.

**From linked questions (6 Qs):**
• The live wire carries the high potential (220 V).
• A fuse is a thin wire of low-melting-point alloy (tin–lead).
• A blown fuse in the live wire isolates the appliance from the 220 V supply.
• T/F: "A fuse wire has a low melting point and melts when excessive current flows through it." → **true** — True.
• Fill: **fuse** — Fuse — melts and breaks the circuit when the current exceeds the safe limit (modern homes use MCBs for the same job).
• A short circuit occurs when the live wire touches the neutral wire directly (e.
teacherTip: Burn a fuse wire with excess current demo (safely) vs show thick wire that won't melt — students grasp why copper substitute kills.
examTip: Fuse rating: slightly above normal working current, well below wiring's safe maximum.

subtopic: 5. Rules for the Safe Use of Electricity
content:
**Executive summary:** Every safety rule hides one physics fact — **conduction through water/skin**, **live-wire potential**, **earthing**, **fuse melting**, or **lightning's path preference**.

**Must know — at home**
• Never touch switches/appliances with **wet hands** (impure water conducts; wet skin low resistance).
• Switches in **LIVE wire** — OFF = fully disconnected from 220 V.
• **Earth** all metal-bodied appliances; three-pin plugs.
• Never thick wire for fuse; switch off **mains** before repair.
• Don't overload sockets; replace frayed wires; keep away from water.
• Pull **plug**, not cord; never poke sockets.

**Thunderstorm / lightning**
• Stay **indoors** or **closed car**; avoid open fields, terraces, swimming.
• **Never** under tall isolated tree — lightning prefers tall isolated conductors.
• Indoors: avoid wired phones; unplug during strike; tall buildings need **lightning conductor**.

**Electric shock rescue**
• Do **NOT** touch victim directly — switch off mains or separate with **dry wooden stick** (insulator).
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Safety rule → physics reason**

| Rule | Physics behind it |
|---|---|
| Dry hands | Wet skin + impure water = **low resistance** path |
| Switch in live wire | OFF cuts **220 V** from appliance |
| Earth metal body | Leakage current to ground, not through **you** |
| Correct fuse wire | **Melts** on overload; copper won't |
| No tree in storm | Lightning hits **tall isolated** conductors |
| Wooden stick rescue | **Insulator** — don't become part of circuit |

**Cornell cues**
• Switch in neutral — safe? → **No** — appliance still at live potential
• Earth wire increases heating power? → **No** — safety only (A-R trap)
• Replace fuse with copper? → **Wrong** — defeats protection
• Closed car in lightning? → Metal shell conducts around occupants

**Definitions / Exam traps**
• **Exam trap** — 'Give two safety rules **with reasons**' — reason carries second mark.
• **Exam trap** — Statement II wrong: thick copper fuse is **dangerous**, not safe practice.
• **Exam trap** — Earthing is **safety**, not power enhancement.

**🔗 Links:** phy-s8-sec_06, phy-s8-sec_05, phy-s8-sec_09

**From linked questions (9 Qs):**
• If the live wire accidentally touches the metal body, the earth wire conducts the leakage current safely into the ground (usually also blowing the fuse).
• If the switch were in the neutral, turning it OFF would stop the current but the appliance would remain connected to the 220 V live wire — touching it could still give a shock.
• Each circuit is rated for a maximum safe current.
• Lightning tends to strike tall, isolated, conducting objects — trees, poles, a person in an open field, water bodies.
• A is true — all metal-bodied appliances are earthed.
• Tap water (with dissolved salts) is a conductor, and wet skin has far lower resistance than dry skin, so even a small leakage can drive a dangerous current through the body.
• I and III are proper precautions.
• T/F: "The switch of an appliance should be connected in the neutral wire." → **false** — False.

**From linked questions (9 Qs):**
• If the live wire accidentally touches the metal body, the earth wire conducts the leakage current safely into the ground (usually also blowing the fuse).
• If the switch were in the neutral, turning it OFF would stop the current but the appliance would remain connected to the 220 V live wire — touching it could still give a shock.
• Each circuit is rated for a maximum safe current.
• Lightning tends to strike tall, isolated, conducting objects — trees, poles, a person in an open field, water bodies.
• A is true — all metal-bodied appliances are earthed.
• Tap water (with dissolved salts) is a conductor, and wet skin has far lower resistance than dry skin, so even a small leakage can drive a dangerous current through the body.
• I and III are proper precautions.
• T/F: "The switch of an appliance should be connected in the neutral wire." → **false** — False.
teacherTip: For every rule, ask 'what physics?' — students who know the reason never forget the rule.
examTip: Two-mark template: Rule + Reason (conduction / potential / earthing / melting point).

content:
**Executive summary:** **Static electricity** arises when rubbing transfers **electrons** between bodies. Charge is **conserved** — equal and opposite on the two bodies.

**Must know — mechanism**
• Only **electrons** move; protons stay in nucleus.
• Body **losing electrons** → **positively charged**.
• Body **gaining electrons** → **negatively charged**.
• Equal and opposite charges — **transferred**, never created/destroyed.

**Standard pairs**
• Glass rod + silk → glass **positive**, silk **negative**.
• Ebonite/plastic + wool/fur → rod **negative**, wool **positive**.

**Everyday static**
• Sparks removing synthetic sweater (dry day).
• Comb through hair picks paper.
• Dust on TV screen; balloon on wall.
• Static builds best in **dry** weather; humid air leaks charge.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Friction charging pairs**

| Rubbing | Body A charge | Body B charge |
|---|---|---|
| Glass rod + silk | Glass **+** | Silk **−** |
| Ebonite/plastic + wool | Rod **−** | Wool **+** |
| Both bodies | Equal magnitude | Opposite sign |

**Cornell cues**
• What moves in charging? → **Electrons only**
• Protons move? → **Never**
• Charge created by friction? → **No** — transferred
• Dry vs humid day for static? → **Dry** better

**Definitions / Exam traps**
• **Static electricity** — accumulation of charge on a body due to friction (no continuous current).
• **Conservation of charge** — total charge unchanged; electrons move from one body to another.
• **Exam trap** — 'Positive charge moved to glass' is **WRONG** — say electrons moved **from** glass.
• **Exam trap** — A–R: equal opposite charges TRUE; electrons created/destroyed FALSE.

**🔗 Links:** phy-s8-sec_10, phy-s8-sec_12, phy-s8-sec_15

**From linked questions (4 Qs):**
• Charging by friction transfers ELECTRONS only — protons stay fixed in the nucleus.
• Rubbing between the synthetic sweater and inner clothing transfers electrons, building opposite static charges.
• A is true: the electrons one body loses, the other gains — equal and opposite charges.
• T/F: "In charging by friction, protons move from one body to the other." → **false** — False.

**From linked questions (4 Qs):**
• Charging by friction transfers ELECTRONS only — protons stay fixed in the nucleus.
• Rubbing between the synthetic sweater and inner clothing transfers electrons, building opposite static charges.
• A is true: the electrons one body loses, the other gains — equal and opposite charges.
• T/F: "In charging by friction, protons move from one body to the other." → **false** — False.
teacherTip: Rub glass with silk in dry weather — hear the crackle. Immediately state electron direction, not 'positive charge movement'.
examTip: Always answer in terms of **electron transfer** — ICSE marks 'protons moved' wrong at every level.

content:
**Executive summary:** **Like charges repel; unlike attract.** Only **repulsion** is the sure test of charge — attraction can involve **uncharged** bodies via **induction**.

**Must know — electrostatic laws**
• Like → repel; unlike → attract.
• **Repulsion** = sure test (both must carry same kind of charge).
• **Attraction** inconclusive — charged body attracts uncharged body too (induced opposite charge on near side).

**Induction attraction (uncharged paper)**
• Comb induces opposite charge on near side of paper; like charge on far side weaker (farther) → net **attraction**.

**Lightning**
• Friction in storm clouds builds huge charges.
• Air insulation breaks down → giant discharge (**lightning**); **thunder** = shock wave from heated air.
• **Lightning conductor** (Franklin): pointed copper spikes → thick strip → plate in soil; discharge follows easy path to ground.
• Franklin's kite — showed lightning is electrical.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Attraction vs repulsion as tests**

| Observation | Proves charging? | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Repulsion** | **Yes — sure test** | Needs like charge on **both** bodies |
| **Attraction** | **Not conclusive** | Charged + uncharged also attract (induction) |
| Two rubbed balloons | **Repel** | Both same charge from hair |

**Cornell cues**
• Sure test of charge? → **Repulsion**
• Comb attracts neutral paper? → **Induction** — opposite charge on near side
• Lightning conductor purpose? → Easy low-resistance path to **ground**
• Thunder is? → Shock wave from suddenly **heated air**

**Definitions / Exam traps**
• **Lightning** — massive electric discharge between charged clouds or cloud and earth.
• **Exam trap** — 'Attraction proves both charged' is FALSE.
• **Exam trap** — 'Why repulsion sure test?' must mention **induction attraction of uncharged bodies**.

**🔗 Links:** phy-s8-sec_12, phy-s8-sec_09, phy-s8-sec_15, phy-s8-sec_07

**From linked questions (11 Qs):**
• Both balloons acquire the SAME kind of charge from the hair, and like charges repel.
• Attraction is inconclusive: a charged body also attracts UNCHARGED objects (by inducing opposite charge on their near side).
• The charged comb rearranges charges in the paper (induction): the side nearer the comb acquires the opposite charge (attracted strongly, being closer) while the far side acquires like charge (repelled weakly, being farther).
• Friction within storm clouds builds enormous charges.
• The pointed copper conductor runs from above the rooftop to a metal plate buried in the earth.
• Attraction can occur with an uncharged body (induced opposite charge on its near side), so attraction proves nothing.
• I and II are correct.
• Attraction is not conclusive: a charged rod also attracts an UNCHARGED ball by inducing opposite charge on its near side.

**From linked questions (11 Qs):**
• Both balloons acquire the SAME kind of charge from the hair, and like charges repel.
• Attraction is inconclusive: a charged body also attracts UNCHARGED objects (by inducing opposite charge on their near side).
• The charged comb rearranges charges in the paper (induction): the side nearer the comb acquires the opposite charge (attracted strongly, being closer) while the far side acquires like charge (repelled weakly, being farther).
• Friction within storm clouds builds enormous charges.
• The pointed copper conductor runs from above the rooftop to a metal plate buried in the earth.
• Attraction can occur with an uncharged body (induced opposite charge on its near side), so attraction proves nothing.
• I and II are correct.
• Attraction is not conclusive: a charged rod also attracts an UNCHARGED ball by inducing opposite charge on its near side.
teacherTip: Two balloons rubbed on same hair repel; each still attracts neutral hair — one demo for repulsion vs attraction logic.
examTip: Repulsion answer must include induction clause — that single phrase carries the mark.

content:
**Executive summary:** **Conduction** = contact → **same** charge acquired; charging body loses charge. **Induction** = no contact → near side gets **opposite** charge; charging body unchanged.

**Must know — conduction (contact)**
• Charged body **touches** uncharged body.
• Charge flows across → body gets **SAME kind** of charge.
• Charging body **loses** some charge.

**Must know — induction (no contact)**
• Charged body brought **near** only.
• Near side → **opposite** charge; far side → like charge.
• Charging body loses **no** charge.
• Permanent charge by induction: rod near → **earth** far side → remove finger → remove rod → body left with charge **opposite** to rod.

**Memory hook**
• **CON**duction = **CON**tact = same **C**harge.
• **IN**duction = no contact = **IN**verted (opposite) charge.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Conduction vs induction**

| | **Conduction** | **Induction** |
|---|---|---|
| Contact? | **Yes** | **No** |
| Charge acquired | **Same** kind | **Opposite** (near side) |
| Charging body charge | **Decreases** | **Unchanged** |
| Permanent charging steps | Just touch | Near → earth → remove earth → remove rod |

**Cornell cues**
• Touch charged rod — what charge? → **Same** as rod
• Rod near metal sphere — near side? → **Opposite** charge
• Charging body loses charge in induction? → **No**
• CONduction mnemonic? → Contact = same Charge

**Definitions / Exam traps**
• **Charging by conduction** — transfer of charge by direct contact.
• **Charging by induction** — redistribution of charge in a body due to nearby charged body without contact.
• **Exam trap** — Standard 3-mark table: contact / kind of charge / loss of charge on charging body.
• **Exam trap** — Fill blank: charging without touching = **induction**.

**🔗 Links:** phy-s8-sec_09, phy-s8-sec_10, phy-s8-sec_15

**From linked questions (3 Qs):**
• Conduction means physical contact: some of the charging body's excess charge flows onto the other body, so both end up with the SAME kind of charge.
• Fill: **induction** — Induction — the near side acquires the opposite charge.
• Conduction: the charged body TOUCHES the uncharged body; some charge flows across, so the body acquires the SAME kind of charge as the charging body, and the charging body loses some of its charge.

**From linked questions (3 Qs):**
• Conduction means physical contact: some of the charging body's excess charge flows onto the other body, so both end up with the SAME kind of charge.
• Fill: **induction** — Induction — the near side acquires the opposite charge.
• Conduction: the charged body TOUCHES the uncharged body; some charge flows across, so the body acquires the SAME kind of charge as the charging body, and the charging body loses some of its charge.
teacherTip: Use pith balls or foil strips: touch vs bring-near — students see same-charge repulsion vs induced attraction pattern.
examTip: Learn the three-row difference table as a table — contact, kind of charge, charging body charge change.

content:
**Executive summary:** A **gold-leaf electroscope** detects charge and compares amounts — like charges on both leaves cause **divergence** by repulsion.

**Must know — construction**
• Brass rod with metal **cap** through insulating stopper in glass case.
• Two thin **gold leaves** hang from rod bottom.

**Detecting charge**
• Charged body touches/nears cap → charge reaches leaves → **like charge** → **repel and diverge**.
• Greater charge → **wider divergence**.

**Identifying nature of unknown charge**
1. Charge electroscope with **known** charge (e.g. positive) — leaves diverge.
2. Bring unknown rod **near** cap (no touch):
   • Divergence **increases** → rod has **SAME** charge.
   • Divergence **decreases** → rod has **opposite** charge (confirm by charging electroscope negatively).
3. Only **increase in divergence** is **conclusive**.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Electroscope responses**

| Rod charge vs electroscope | Effect on leaves | Conclusive? |
|---|---|---|
| **Same** as electroscope | Divergence **increases** | **Yes** |
| **Opposite** | Divergence **decreases** | Suggestive — confirm with reverse charging |
| **Uncharged** | May cause slight change | **Not** conclusive |
| More charge on electroscope | Wider divergence | Detects **amount** roughly |

**Cornell cues**
• Leaves diverge because? → **Like charges repel**
• Positive rod near positive electroscope? → Divergence **increases**
• Sure indication of charge nature? → **Increase** in divergence only
• Touch cap vs bring near for testing? → **Near** for nature test

**Definitions / Exam traps**
• **Electroscope** — instrument to detect and test electric charge (gold leaves repel).
• **Exam trap** — State conclusive rule explicitly: 'increase in divergence is sure indication' — upgrades to full marks.
• **Exam trap** — Decrease alone not conclusive — uncharged conductor or hand approaching can also decrease divergence.

**🔗 Links:** phy-s8-sec_10, phy-s8-sec_12, phy-s8-sec_09

**From linked questions (5 Qs):**
• Charge travels from the cap down the metal rod to both gold leaves.
• A positive rod near the cap repels positive charge down into the leaves, increasing their positive charge and divergence.
• T/F: "In a gold-leaf electroscope, the divergence of the leaves increases with the amount of charge given to it." → **true** — True.
• Fill: **electroscope** — Electroscope (gold-leaf electroscope) — like charges on the two leaves make them repel and diverge.
• 1.

subtopic: 10. Solving Numericals: Electrical Energy and Cost
content:
**Executive summary:** **Energy (kWh) = power (kW) × time (h)**. Bill charges **units** (kWh) × rate.

**Must know — formulas**
• Energy (kWh) = power (kW) × time (h)
• **1 kWh = 1 unit** = 3.6 × 10⁶ J
• **Cost = units × rate per unit**

**Worked patterns**
• 2 kW heater × 3 h = **6 units**/day; at ₹6/unit → **₹36**.
• 100 W × 10 h = 0.1 kW × 10 h = **1 unit**.
• 2000 W geyser × 30 min = 2 kW × 0.5 h = **1 unit**; at ₹8/unit → **₹8**.

**Checklist**
• W → kW: **÷ 1000**
• Minutes → hours: **÷ 60**
• 'Units' on bill **always** means kWh
• Monthly cost: daily units × days, then × rate
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Unit conversion traps**

| Given | Convert to | Common error |
|---|---|---|
| 100 W | 0.1 kW (÷1000) | Writing 100 kW |
| 30 minutes | 0.5 h (÷60) | Using 30 as hours |
| 2000 W | 2 kW | Forgetting conversion |
| Result in kWh | = 'units' on meter | Confusing with kW |

**📊 Chart — Numerical workflow**

| Step | Action | Example (2 kW, 3 h, ₹6) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Power in kW | 2 kW |
| 2 | Time in h | 3 h |
| 3 | Energy = kW × h | 6 kWh = 6 units |
| 4 | Cost = units × rate | 6 × ₹6 = ₹36 |

**Cornell cues**
• 1 kWh in joules? → **3.6 × 10⁶ J**
• 100 W, 10 h → units? → **1 unit**
• Bill charges for? → **Energy** (kWh), not power
• Convert **before** multiplying

**Definitions / Exam traps**
• **Unit of electricity** — commercial name for 1 kWh on the meter.
• **Exam trap** — All errors are unit errors — convert W and minutes FIRST.
• **Exam trap** — ICSE: one line per step (kW × h = kWh = units; × rate = ₹) — mark per line.

**🔗 Links:** phy-s8-sec_03, phy-s8-sec_02, phy-s8-sec_05
